# Horizontal Mergers and Innovation in Concentrated Industries
Brett Hollenbeck, UCLA Anderson School of Management
2020

## What is this paper about?

This paper asks whether allowing dominant firms to acquire smaller rivals is ultimately helpful or harmful to long-run innovation and consumer welfare. The core finding is that even when horizontal mergers are purely anti-competitive in the short run — reducing competition, raising prices, and offering no cost efficiencies — they can, under the right conditions, dramatically increase the long-run rate of innovation and consumer welfare. The mechanism is preemptive or speculative entry: the prospect of a lucrative buyout induces new firms to enter the industry and invest in product quality who otherwise would not, generating substantially more competition and innovation than a world without mergers. The paper develops a dynamic oligopoly model with fully endogenous entry, exit, investment, and mergers to study this mechanism and the conditions under which it holds. For mergers to increase innovation entry costs must be low, entrants and incumbents must both have the ability to innovate rapidly, and the degree of horizontal product differentiation must be low. 

## Important context

- This is a theoretical/computational paper, not empirical. There is no dataset; all results come from numerical solutions of a dynamic oligopoly model solved via a modified stochastic algorithm.
- "Innovation" here means advancement of an industry's technological quality frontier, measured as the rate at which firms push product quality beyond the current maximum.
- The model deliberately stacks the deck against mergers benefiting consumers statically (no cost efficiencies, products are removed from market) in order to isolate the dynamic innovation channel.
- The model is an Ericson-Pakes quality-ladder framework extended to allow endogenous mergers, a flexible long-run innovation rate (following Goettler-Gordon 2014), and the MNS&W investment technology that is "merger neutral."
- The paper also contributes a modified stochastic algorithm (epsilon-greedy exploration from reinforcement learning) that substantially improves convergence of the Pakes-McGuire computational method.
- Results are presented across a wide range of parameter values — not just the benchmark case — to characterize the conditions under which mergers increase versus decrease welfare.

## Data and methods

- **Model:** Dynamic oligopoly, Ericson-Pakes quality ladder; firms produce vertically differentiated goods and compete in prices (logit demand).
- **Investment technology:** MNS&W "capital/quality augmentation" — stochastic cost draws, merger-neutral by construction.
- **Merger stage:** Random-order proposal game embedded each period; bilateral Nash bargaining over buyout price; mergers endogenous.
- **Computation:** Modified Pakes-McGuire stochastic algorithm with epsilon-greedy exploration; solved numerically for symmetric Markov Perfect Equilibria in pure strategies; simulated for 500,000 periods per equilibrium.
- **State space:** Product quality mapped to integers {0,...,10}; up to 4 active firms; parameters drawn from Pakes-McGuire (1994) benchmark.
- **Parameter exploration:** Simple Continuation Method traces equilibrium outcomes as entry costs, entrant quality cap, innovation capacity, synergy level, and horizontal differentiation vary.

## Key results

1. **Allowing mergers can dramatically increase the innovation rate:** In the benchmark case, the rate of innovation (advancement of the quality frontier per period) increases from 0.35 without mergers to 0.82 with mergers — a 2.3× increase. Average consumer surplus rises from 7.75 to 10.02.

2. **Preemptive entry is the mechanism:** With mergers, entry rates increase from 11% to 27% of periods. Entry occurs at every market state, including when 2–3 incumbents are present, ruling out the standard "replacement entry" story from the Horizontal Merger Guidelines.

3. **Very specific conditions are necessary for the result to hold:** (a) Entry costs must be low enough to make speculative entry profitable. (b) Entrants must be capable of entering at middling quality — if entrants can only reach the bottom of the quality ladder, mergers remain harmful. (c) Incumbents must be capable of generating rapid (large) quality improvements. (d) Horizontal product differentiation must be low (markets must be contestable).

4. **Synergistic mergers amplify the effect:** When merging firms can combine products (parameter σ), innovation and consumer welfare increase even more steeply as σ rises. Even modest synergies generate significant long-run consumer benefits through higher innovation.

5. **Policy implication:** Traditional merger review based on static welfare analysis may be counterproductive in innovative industries. Antitrust authorities should instead focus on barriers to entry and contestability — restricting long-term contracts, bundling, and switching costs may do more to promote innovation than strict merger review.

## Limitations and scope

- **Theory only:** The mechanism is shown to exist under a range of parameters but not verified empirically. There is no direct test of whether real-world industries exhibit the entry-for-buyout pattern.
- **Parameter sensitivity:** The main result reverses when entry costs are high, entrants are limited to low quality, or innovation is slow and incremental. The conditions under which mergers benefit consumers are well-characterized but may not be satisfied in many industries.
- **Homogeneous goods not covered:** The model assumes differentiated goods competing in prices. Results need not apply to Cournot settings with homogeneous goods.
- **Computational constraints:** The model caps firms at 4 active in equilibrium and quality at 10 levels; results may not generalize to industries with many more competitors or a larger quality range.
- **No empirical analog:** The model abstracts from many real-world features — firm heterogeneity, multi-product firms, asymmetric costs, specific industry data. Applying the results to policy requires industry-specific analysis.
- **Single equilibrium reported:** Multiple MPE can exist. The Simple Continuation Method provides evidence against meaningfully different equilibria, but multiplicity cannot be ruled out.

## Navigation guide

- **Read first:** Abstract and Introduction for the core argument and the preemptive entry mechanism; Section 4.1 and Table 2 for the benchmark comparison of mergers vs. no-mergers outcomes.
- **For the mechanism:** Figure 4 (Industry Dynamics Following a Merger) and Figure 5 (Entry Rates) show how entry responds to the merger regime.
- **For the model:** Section 2 (Model) for the investment and merger stage specifications; Section 3 for equilibrium definition and the computational algorithm.
- **For conditions on when mergers help vs. hurt:** Section 5 — entry costs (Section 5.1, Figure 6), synergies (Section 5.2, Figure 7), entrant quality (Section 5.3, Figure 8), incumbent innovation capacity (Section 5.4, Table 4), horizontal differentiation (Section 5.5, Table 5).
- **For the computational algorithm:** Appendix A, including the epsilon-greedy modification.

## Publication status

Published: *Quantitative Marketing and Economics*, 2020, Vol. 18, pp. 1–37.
https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s11129-019-09218-2
https://doi.org/10.1007/s11129-019-09218-2
https://papers.ssrn.com/sol3/papers.cfm?abstract_id=2621842
